sourceQuestion:
Consider two events E1 and E2 such that P(E1) = 1/2, P(E2) = 1/3, and P(E1 intersect E2) = 1/5. Which of the following statements is/are true?

A. P(E1 union E2) = 19/30
B. Events E1 and E2 are independent
C. Events E1 and E2 are not independent
D. P(E1 | E2) = 4/5
sourceAnswer: C. Events E1 and E2 are not independent

parsed.stem: Consider two events E1 and E2 such that P(E1) = 1/2, P(E2) = 1/3, and P(E1 intersect E2) = 1/5. Which of the following statements is/are true?

options.text: Events E1 and E2 are independent
Events E1 and E2 are not independent

sourceQuestion:

- P(E1) + P(E2) = 1
- P(E1 intersect E2) = 1
- E1 and E2 are independent

The value of P(E1), the probability of the event E1, is

A. 0
B. 1/4
C. 1/2
D. 1

sourceQuestion:
Consider a random experiment where two fair coins are tossed. Let A be the event that denotes HEAD on both the throws, B be the event that denotes HEAD on the first throw, and C be the event that denotes HEAD on the second throw. Which of the following statements is/are TRUE?

A. A and B are independent.
B. A and C are independent.
C. B and C are independent.
D. Prob(B | C) = Prob(B)

parsed.stem: Consider a random experiment where two fair coins are tossed. Let A be the event that denotes HEAD on both the throws, B be the event that denotes HEAD on the first throw, and C be the event that denotes HEAD on the second throw. Which of the following statements is/are TRUE?

sourceQuestion:
Consider a coin-toss experiment where the probability of head showing up is p. In the i-th coin toss, let X_i = 1 if head appears, and X_i = 0 if tail appears. Consider S = sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i where n is the total number of independent coin tosses. Which of the following statements is/are correct?

A. E[p] = p
B. E[p] = P
C. As n increases, variance of p decreases
D. Variance of p does not depend on n

parsed.stem: Consider a coin-toss experiment where the probability of head showing up is p. In the i-th coin toss, let X_i = 1 if head appears, and X_i = 0 if tail appears. Consider S = sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i where n is the total number of independent coin tosses. Which of the following statements is/are correct?

options.text: As n increases, variance of p decreases
Variance of p does not depend on n

sourceQuestion:
Three fair coins are tossed independently. T is the event that two or more tosses result in heads. S is the event that two or more tosses result in tails. What is the probability of the event T intersection S?

A. 0
B. 0.5
C. 0.25
D. 1

parsed.stem: Three fair coins are tossed independently. T is the event that two or more tosses result in heads. S is the event that two or more tosses result in tails. What is the probability of the event T intersection S?

sourceQuestion:
Let X be a normal distribution with mean -1 and variance 4. Let Y be another normal variable with mean 1 and variance unknown. If P(X <= -1) = P(Y >= 2), what is the variance of Y?

A. 3
B. 2
C. sqrt(2)
D. 1

parsed.stem: Let X be a normal distribution with mean -1 and variance 4. Let Y be another normal variable with mean 1 and variance unknown. If P(X <= -1) = P(Y >= 2), what is the variance of Y?

sourceQuestion:
Suppose p is the number of cars per minute passing through a certain road junction between 5 PM and 6 PM, and p has a Poisson distribution with mean 3. What is the probability of observing fewer than 3 cars during any given minute in this interval?

A. 8/(2e^3)
B. 9/(2e^3)
C. 17/(2e^3)
D. 26/(2e^3)

parsed.stem: Suppose p is the number of cars per minute passing through a certain road junction between 5 PM and 6 PM, and p has a Poisson distribution with mean 3. What is the probability of observing fewer than 3 cars during any given minute in this interval?

sourceQuestion:
In a multi-user operating system on an average, 20 requests are made to use a particular resource per hour. The arrival of requests follows a Poisson distribution. The probability that either one, three or five requests are made in 30 minutes is given by:
A. 6.5 * 10^5 * e^-20
B. 1.02 * 10^-5 * e^-10
C. 17 / (3*2^3)
D. 26 / (3*2^5)

sourceQuestion:
The probability that it will rain today is 0.5. The probability that it will rain tomorrow is 0.6. The probability that it will rain either today or tomorrow is 0.7. What is the probability that it will rain today and tomorrow?

A. 0.3
B. 0.25
C. 0.35
D. 0.4

D. 3/4

sourceQuestion:
Seven (distinct) car accidents occurred in a week. What is the probability that they all occurred on the same day?

A. 1/7
B. 1/7^6
C. 1/(2*7^6)
D. 7/2^6

A. 3/8
B. 1/2
C. 5/8
D. 3/4

sourceQuestion:
Consider a company that assembles computers. The probability of a faulty assembly of any computer is p. The company therefore subjects each computer to a testing process. This testing process gives the correct result for any computer with a probability of q. What is the probability of a computer being declared faulty?

A. pq + (1-p)(1-q)
B. (1-q)p
C. (1-p)q
D. pq

parsed.stem: Consider a company that assembles computers. The probability of a faulty assembly of any computer is p. The company therefore subjects each computer to a testing process. This testing process gives the correct result for any computer with a probability of q. What is the probability of a computer being declared faulty?

A. 1/625
B. 4/625
C. 12/625
D. 16/625

sourceQuestion:
A deck of 52 cards (each carrying a distinct number from 1 to 52) is shuffled thoroughly. Two cards are then removed one at a time from the deck. What is the probability that the two cards are selected with the number on the first card being one higher than the number on the second card?

A. 1/52
B. 2/52
C. 1/4
D. 1/2

sourceQuestion:
Let b_n be the number of n-bit strings that do NOT contain two consecutive 1s. Which one of the following is the recurrence relation for b_n?

A. b_n = b_{n-1} + b_{n-2}, b_1 = 1, b_2 = 2
B. b_n = b_{n-1} + b_{n-2}, b_1 = 2, b_2 = 3
C. b_n = 2*b_{n-1}, b_1 = 1
D. b_n = b_{n-1} + 2*b_{n-2}, b_1 = 1

parsed.stem: Let b_n be the number of n-bit strings that do NOT contain two consecutive 1s. Which one of the following is the recurrence relation for b_n?

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following recurrence:

f(1) = 1;
f(2n) = 2*f(n) - 1,  for n >= 1;
f(2n+1) = 2*f(n) + 1,  for n >= 1.

Then, which of the following statements is/are TRUE?

A. f(2^n - 1) = 2^n - 1  for all n >= 1
B. f(2^n) = 2^(n-1)  for all n >= 1
C. f(2^n + 1) = 3  for all n >= 1
D. f(2^n) = 1  for all n >= 1

The Lucas sequence L_n is defined by the recurrence relation:

L_n = L_{n-1} + L_{n-2},  for n >= 3,

with L_1 = 1 and L_2 = 3.

Which one of the options given is TRUE?

A. L_n = ((1 + sqrt(5))/2)^n - ((1 - sqrt(5))/2)^n
B. L_n = ((1 + sqrt(5))/2)^n + ((1 - sqrt(5))/2)^n
C. L_n = F_n + F_{n+2}  where F_n is the standard Fibonacci number (F_1=F_2=1)
D. L_n = F_n + 2*F_{n-1}  where F_0 = 0, F_1 = 1

parsed.stem:
The Lucas sequence L_n is defined by the recurrence relation:

L_n = L_{n-1} + L_{n-2},  for n >= 3,

with L_1 = 1 and L_2 = 3.

Which one of the options given is TRUE?

options.text: L_n = F_n + F_{n+2} where F_n is the standard Fibonacci number (F_1=F_2=1)

sourceQuestion:
Let H_1, H_2, H_3, ... be harmonic numbers defined by H_n = sum_{j=1}^{n} 1/j.

Then, for n >= 1, sum_{i=1}^{n} H_i can be expressed as:

A. (n+1)*H_n - n
B. n*H_n
C. (n+1)*H_{n+1} - n
D. n*H_{n+1} - n

Let H_1, H_2, H_3, ... be harmonic numbers defined by H_n = sum_{j=1}^{n} 1/j.

sourceQuestion:
Consider the sequence (x_n) defined by the recurrence relation x_{n+1} = c*x_n^2 - 2, where c > 0. Suppose there exists a non-empty, open interval (a, b) such that for all x_0 satisfying a < x_0 < b, the sequence converges to a limit. The sequence converges to the value:

A. (1 + sqrt(1 + 8c)) / (2c)
B. (1 - sqrt(1 + 8c)) / (2c)
C. 2
D. 1/sqrt(c)

sourceQuestion:
A bag has r red balls and b black balls. All balls are identical except for their colours. In a trial, a ball is randomly drawn from the bag, its colour is noted and the ball is placed back into the bag along with another ball of the same colour. Note that the number of balls in the bag will increase by one after each trial. A sequence of four such trials is conducted. What is the probability of drawing a red ball in the fourth trial?

A. r / (r + b)
B. (r + 3) / (r + b + 3)
C. (r + b + 3) / r
D. r / (r + b + 3)

parsed.stem: A bag has r red balls and b black balls. All balls are identical except for their colours. In a trial, a ball is randomly drawn from the bag, its colour is noted and the ball is placed back into the bag along with another ball of the same colour. Note that the number of balls in the bag will increase by one after each trial. A sequence of four such trials is conducted. What is the probability of drawing a red ball in the fourth trial?

sourceQuestion:
Let A and B be two events in a probability space with P(A) = 0.3, P(B) = 0.5, and P(A | B) = 0.1. Which of the following statements is/are TRUE?

A. The two events A and B are independent and P(A | B) = 0.3 and P(B | A) = 0.5, respectively.
B. P(A union B) = 0.75
C. P(A^c | B) = 0.9, where A^c is the complement of the event A
D. P(A | B^c) = P(A | B), where A^c and B^c are the complements of the events A and B respectively

= 0.3 and P(B | A) = 0.5, respectively. B. P(A union B) = 0.75
P(A^c | B) = 0.9, where A^c is the complement of the event A
P(A | B^c) = P(A | B), where A^c and B^c are the complements of the events A and B respectively

sourceQuestion:
In a population of N families, 30% of the families have three children, 20% of the families have two children and the remaining families have one child. What is the probability that a randomly picked child belongs to a family with two children?
A. 1/5
B. 2/9
C. 3/10
D. 2/3

parsed.stem: In a population of N families, 30% of the families have three children, 20% of the families have two children and the remaining families have one child. What is the probability that a randomly picked child belongs to a family with two children?
